posts.title: 1. Introduction to Rheology
posts.text: 1. Chemical bonding and molecular interactions98% H, C, N, O, P, SOthers: Na, Mg, K, Ca, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, ZnEven smaller amount: Cr, Mo, W, Se, I There are many types of bonds Covalent bonds Ionic bonds Hydrogen bonds : Occur between polar molecules, such as molecules of water, because of the partial negative charge on the O and the partial postive charge on the H. Hydrophobic interactions : In aqueous environment, the hydrophobic molecules tend to aggregate with one another rather than interact with polar water molecules. (물 분자와 잘 상호작용하지 않는 비극성 분자들이 물 분자와의 접촉을 최소화하기 위해 서로 뭉치는 경향에서 발생) Van Der Waals forces (VDW) : Occur when atoms of two molecules are in close proximity. (분자 전체의 순간적인 쌍극자 모멘트) 1.1 Covalent bonds involving H, C, N, O, P and SValence shell complete with two electrons: One covalent bondCarbon : electronsNitrogen : electronsOxygen : electronsValence shell complete with 8 electrons Primary SequenceProtein length: 40AAPeptides length: 2 - 39AA Aminoacid combined and become Peptides bonds. (Dipeptide)and then by convention(합침) -> H2O come out!And directionality is also really Important! (합치는 순서에 따라 완전히 다는 것이 만들어짐.) 1.2 Biological macromoleculesAll the cells have lipids, carbonhydrates, nucleic acids and proteins. 1.3 Lipids (지질)Lipids or fats are predominately hydrocarbon chains that are used as energy storage and insulation. Lipids are hydrophobic in nature. Cell membranes are composed of liquid bilayers, which seperate the aqueous inside of the cell from the aqueous outside of the cell. 1.4 ProteinsProteins are linear chains of amino acids of which there are 20 distinct types. The order of the amino acids in the chain dictates the shape and the function of the protein in the cell. Protein Folding (Protein structure) is described by: Primary Sequence : Based on covalent(Peptides Bonds) Secondary Structure ; -> Based on Non-Covalent(공유결합 X) interactions (helix sturcture) They are keys of non-covalent forces Tertiary Structure (접힘 구조); -> Based on Non-Covalent(공유결합 X) interactionsEx) Side chain - side chain, peptide “backbone”, water Quaternary Structure ; -> Based on Non-Covalent(공유결합 X) interactions The polypeptide chain of protein is always written in an N -> C direction (biosynthesized in an N -> C direction.)

posts.title: 1. Enzymes & Metabolism
posts.text: 1. EnzymesEnzymes are protein based biological catalysts. TypesISOZYME : Same reaction on different gene MOSTLY USEDALLLOZYME : same gene with variations If it ends with “ASE” then it might be a enzyme. ex) Protease G = Gibbs free energyH = EnthalpyT = KS = Entropy Enzyme Inhibitors? Why are they useful?Competitive Inhibitors! Carbohydrate (ends with “OSE”)Glucose (25%) C6H12O6 = CH2O 2. Nucleotides and Nucleic AcidsExamples of common nucleotides Adenosine triphosphate 3’-5’-cyclic adenosine Guanosine triphosphate (GTP) 3. CarbohydratesCarbohydrates(탄수화물): Carbohydrates include monosachharides, disaccharides, trisaccharide or polysaccharides

posts.title: 1. Introduction to Rheology
posts.text: 1.1 What is Rheology?Rheology is the science that deals with the way materials deform when forces are applied to them. (재료에 힘이 가해졌을 때 재료가 어떻게 변형되는지)So the two key terms of rehology are deformation and force. Two principal aspects of rheology.One involves the development of quantitative relationships between deformation and force for a material of interest.첫 번째는 관심 있는 재료에 대해 변형과 힘 사이의 정량적 관계를 개발하는 것 The information for the development of such a relationship is obtained from experimental measurements.두 번째 측면은 물질의 구조와 조성, 온도 및 압력이 레이올로지적 거동에 어떻게 영향을 미치는지를 보여주는 관계를 파악하는 것 Molten plastics are rheologically complex materials that cen exhibit both viscous flow and elastic recoil. Polymers that melt and are processed as liquids are called thermoplastics.In an extruder, the dependence of viscosity on shear rate and temperature is of most interest, but once a melt leaves a die, its viscoelastic properties govern its behavior.Rheological data are also useful for polymer characterization. Normal Stress Compressive Stress Tensile Stress Shear Stress In the case of simple extension, there is only one significant feature of the stress field, because there are no shear stress acting, and there is axial symmetry. This is the principal stretching stress , which is a normal stress difference, the difference between the tensile stress and the ambient pressure surrounding the sample. Because of the relatively high resistance to deformation of molten, high-molecular-weight polymers, the tensile stress driving the deformation is normally much larger than the ambient pressure. Thus, 1.4 Stain: A Measure of Deformation1.4.1 Stain Measures for Simple ExtensionWe can easily form such a quantity by dividing the length difference by the initial length to obtain the engineering or linear strain for smple or unaxial extention. Hencky strainExtra \left or missing \right\epsilon = \ln \left (\frac{dX_1(t)}{dX_1(t_0)}right) 1.4.2 A Strain Measure for ShearAn obvisous choice of a strain mearue is the displacement of the moving plate, , divided by the distance between the plates, h. The shear rate is simply the rate of change of the shear strain with time:where V is the velocity of the moving plate. 1.5 Rheological Phenomena1.5.1 Elasticity: Hooke’s LawThe constant of proportionality E is called Young’s modulus, and this relationship is called Hooke’s law.The corresponding form (동치) of Hooke’s law for simple shear is typically expressed as: Where: is the shear stress is the shear modulus (a material property) is the shear strain 1.5.2 Viscosity: The Newtonian FluidViscosity is the property of a material that describes its resistance to continuous deformation. Unlike elasticity, the stress is not related to the amount of deformation but to the rate of deformation. A linear relationship between the shear stress and the shear rate: Where is the viscosity.A material that obeys this equation, with the viscosity independent of shear rate, is called a Newtonian fluid. The quantity measured in uniaxial extension is the net tensile stress . We will see later that the extensional viscosity is defined as so that for a Newtonian fluid, the extensional viscosity is just three times the viscosity. 1.5.2 ViscoelasticityThe total force F is the sum of the force in the

posts.title: 6. Diffusion
posts.text: 1. IntroductionThe phenomenon of diffusion may be demonstrated with the use of a diffusion couple, which is formed by joining bars of two different metals together so that there is intimate contact between the two faces. The types of diffusion:Interdiffusion (= inpurity diffusion)Self-diffusion 2. Diffusion MechanismsFor an atom to make such a move, two conditions must be met:(1) There must be an empty adjacent site.(2) The atom must have sufficient energy (vibrational energy) to break bonds with its neighbor atoms and then cause some lattice distortion during the displacement. Vacancy DiffusionAn atom from a normal lattice position to an adjacent vacant lattice site or vacancy, as represented schematically. Interstitial DiffusionThis mechanism is found for interdiffusion of impurities such as hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, which have atoms that are small enough to into the interstitial positions. 3. Fick’s First LawDiffusion is a time-dependent process- that is, in a macroscopic sense, the quantity of an element that is transported within another is a function of time.Often it is necessary to know how fast diffusion occurs, or the rate of mass transfer.The rate is frequently expressed as diffusion flux(J), defined as the mass M diffusing through and perpendicular to a unit cross-sectional area of solid per unit of time. <1> Quantitative definition M: The massA: The area across which diffusion is occuringt: The elapsed(경과된) difffusion time <2> Theoretical model Fick’s First lawRelationship between diffusion flux and concentration gradient! D: The diffusion coefficient The constant of proportionality(비례상수) D is called diffusion coefficient, which is expressed in square meters per second. The negative sign in this expression indicates that the direction of diffusion is down the concentration gradient, from a high to a low concentration. The mass of diffusing species entering the plate on the high-pressure side is equal to the mass exiting from the low-pressure surface (즉, 높은 압력 측에서 판에 들어오는 확산 물질의 질량이 낮은 압력 측에서 나가는 질량과 같음) - such that there is no net accumulation of diffusing species in the plate. This is an example of what is termed steady-state diffusion. Concentration CThe concentration gradient is the slope at a particular point on this curve. In many diffusion scenarios, this is assumed to be linear for simplicity, as shown in the equation above. in this Equation, the concentration gradient is the driving force. 4. Fick’s Second Law - Nonsteady-State DiffusionNonsteady-State Diffusion, where the concentraion C (농도) of a diffusing species changes with both time t and position x. D : The diffusion coefficient, which measures how easily the species diffuses through the medium. It may depend on position x : The derivative with respect to position, showing how the concentration changes spatially. :The concentration gradient (how the concentration changes with respect to position). Boundary ConditionsFor t > 0, C = Cs (the constant surface concentration) at x = 0Fort>0, atx=∞ 5. Factors that Influence DiffusionDiffusing SpeciesSelf-diffusion occurs by a vacancy mechanism, whereas carbon diffusion in iron is interstitial. TemperatureThe temperature dependence of the diffusion coefficients is D: is the diffusion coefficient : is a constant that is independent of temperature : is the activation energy for diffusion (J/mol or eV/atom) : is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) : is the absolute temperature 6. Diffusion in Semiconducting MaterialsOne technology that applies solid-state diffusion is the fabrication(공정) of semiconductor integrated circuits (ICs). Each integrated circuit chis

posts.title: 5. Imperfections in Solids
posts.text: 1. IntroductionIdealized solid does not exist! 2. Point Defects in MetalsvacancyThe simplest of the point defects is a vacancy, or vacant lattice site, one normally occupied but from which an atom is missing. The necessity of the existence of vacancies is explained using principles of thermodynamics. The presence of vacancies increases the entropy of the crystal.The equilibrium number of vacancies : The number of vacancies (empty lattice sites). : The total number of atomic sites in the crystal. : Energy required to form a vacancy, expressed in or k: Boltxmann’s constant 1.38 × 10−23 J/atom ∙ K, or 8.62 × 10−5 eV/atom ∙ K T: Absolute temperature Self-interstitialis an atom from the crystal that is crowded into an interstitial site - a small void space that under ordinary circumstances is not occupied. *Determine the value of Nex) the number of atomic sites per cubic meter for copper. 3. Point Defects in CeramicsWhen defect structures are considered conditions of electroneutrality must be maintained. Frenkel defectOne such type of defect involves a cation-vacancy and a cation-interstitial pair. Schottky defectA cation vacancy - anion vacancy pair. The ratio of cations to anions is not altered by the formation of either a Frenkel or a Schottky defect. If no other defects are present, the material is said to be stoichiometric(화학양론적). Nonstoichiometry may occur for some ceramic materials in which two valance states exiest for one of the ion types. Upper side we calculated the differences between Number of vacancy and Frekel efects are and devided by 2kT not kT For vacancies , we use because creating a vacancy involves just one defect. For Frenkel and Schottky defects ), we use because each defect formation involves two defects, distributing the energy cost across both. Frenkel Defects Schottky Defects 4. Impurities in SolidsThe addition of impurity atoms to a metal results in the formation of solid solution and/or a new second phase, depending on the kinds of impurity, their concentrations, and the temperature of the alloy. The present discussion is concerned with the notion of a solid solution.Regard to alloys, solute 용질 and solvent 용매 are terms that are commonly employed. Solvent is the element or compound that is present in the greatest amount; on occasion, solvent atoms are also called host atoms. Solid Solutionsimpurity point defects are found in solid solutions, of which there are two types: substitutional and interstitial. For the substitutional type, solute or impurity atoms replace or substitute for the host atoms. Several fetures of the solute and solvent atoms determine the degree to which the former dissolves in the latter. These are expressed as four Hume-Rothery rules. Atomic size factorAppreciable(상당한) quantities of a solute may be accommodated in this type of solid solution only when the difference in atomic radii between the two atom types is less than about +-15%. Crystal structureThe crystal structures for metals of both atom types must be the same. Electronegativity factorThe more electropositive one element and the more electronegative the other, the greater the likelihood that they will form an intermatallic compound instead of a substitutional solid solution. So the smaller the difference in electronegativity, the more likely they are to form a solid solution. ValencesOther factors being equal, a metal has more of a tendency to dissolve another metal of higher valency than to dissolve one of a lower valency. An example of substitutional solid solution is found for copper and nickel. With regard to the aforementioned rules that govern degree of solubility, the atomic radii for copper and nickel are 0.128 and 0.125nm, respectively. Both have the FCC crystal structure. For interstital solid solutions, impurity atoms fill the voids or interstices among the host atoms. For both FCC and BCC crystal structures! Tetrahedral: sites have a coordination number of 4; Octahedral: sites the coordination number is 6; In FCC structures, there are two types of octahedral sites, represented by point coordinates and . The representative coordinate for a single type of tetrahedral site is . Figure 5.6a shows the locations of these sites within the FCC unit cell.For BCC, there is one type of each octahedral and tetrahedral site. The representative coordinates are: Octahedral: Tetrahedral: Impurities in CeramicsSolid solutions of both substitutional and interstital types are possible. For interstitial, the ionic radius of the impurity must be relatively small in comparison to the anion. 7. Dislocations - Linear DefectsEdge dislocationA dislocation is a linear or one-dimensional defect around which some of the atoms are misaligned. Screw dislocationIt’s being formed by a shear stress that is applied to produce the distortion. Mixed dislocationsMost dislocations found in crystalline are probably neither pure edge nor pure screw but exhibit components of both types. Burgers vector 9. Bulk or Volume DefectsThat are much larger than those heretofore discussed. 10. Atomic VibrationsEvery atom in a solid material is vibrating very rapidly about its lattice position within the crystal. In a sense, these atomic vibrations may be thought of as imperfections or defects. 11. Basic Concepts of MicroscopyMacroscopic Structure: They are large enough to be observed with the unaided eye. Microscopic Structure includes the size and shape of 12. Microscopic TechniquesOptical MicroscopyNormally, careful and meticulous surface preparations are necessary to reveal the important details of the microsturcture. Electron Microscopy(전자 현미경) Modes of Operation Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)The image seen with a trasmission electron microscope (TEM) is formed by an electron beam that passes through the specimen. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)More useful! The surface of a specimen to be examined is scanned with an electron beam, and the reflected beam of electrons is collected and displazed at the same scanning rate on a cathode ray tube. The surface may or may not be polished and etched, but it must be electrically conductive! A very thin metallic surface coating must be applied to nonconductive materials. Scanning Probe MicroscopyScanning probe microscopes employ a tiny probe with a very sharp tip that is brought into very close proximity of the specimen surface. 13.Grain Size DeterminationGrain Size Determination Techniques: Linear interceptcounting numbers of grain boundary intersections by straight test lines.P: the total number of intersectionsL: the total length of all the lines The mean intercept lengthM: Magnification (measured scale length (converted to microns)/ the number appearing by the scale bar (in microns)) Comparison - comparing grain structures with standardized charts, which are based upon grain areas.(i.e., number of grains per unit area). G: ASTM grain-size number For photomicrographs taken at magnifications other than 100×, the following modified version of Equation must be used:

posts.title: 4. Entropic self-assembly
posts.text: 1. EntropyThe entropy S denotes the numbr of microstates of a system:Examples:N-fold coin toss EXAMPLEA pre-progremmed nanoparticle release cascadeAim #1: The nanoparticle (NP, for example, a drug carrier) should only be released, if the carrier matrix gets in contact with the human body.Aim #2: NP-species should be released sequentially, and from bottom to top mechanism #1: when incubated with DNA strands, silver nanoparticles (cationic) form microggregates which cannot escape from the gel matrix. However, these stable aggregates can be disintegrated by the addition of NaCl. Then, not only the silver NPs but also the DNA strands are mobilized. Part 2: amyloid-associated diseases, Debye-screening and the Hofmeister-effect-synuclein is mainly found in nerve tissue. However, its physiolocial(생리학적) role is still unknown. It occurs in very high concentrations up to 1% of the entire protein content of neurons Lewy bodies are detected in the neural tissue of almost all patients suffering from Parkinson’s disease. Still, the type of aggregate that is responsible for pathological alterations, is yet to be identified.

posts.title: 2. Quick overview of the finite element method
posts.text:
1. Finite Element Method, FEMThe finite element method is a rich and versatile approach to construct computational schemes to solve any partial differential equation on any domain in any dimension. Consider the following PDE(Partial Differential Equation) in 2D: with boundary conditions, for reasons of mathematical clarity and convenience. REVIEW The Laplacian In 2D, it defined as The Laplacian measures the “curvature” or “rate of change of the rate of change” of the function u(x, y). It is a key operator in physics and engineering, as it captures how u(x, y) changes across space. Why The equation has various physical and mathematical interpretations:Poisson’s equation: represents a source termLaplace’s equation: , the equation becomes the Laplace equation, which describes equilibrium statesThe negatie sign(-) is used for physical reasons. A finite difference scheme to solve the current PDE would in the simplest case be described by the stencil Example) Challenges in Constructing Stentils for General PDEsThe solution u on an element is expressed as a polynomial combination of basis functions:The PDE is rewritten in its variational form enabling element-wise integrationto produce the stencil. Representation of the SolutionThe expression: : These are unknowns to be solved for. Their number N+1 represents the degrees of freedom of the element. (는 미지수로 계산을 통해 구해야하며, 의 개수 N+1은 해당 요소의 자유도를 나타낸다.) : These are predefined polynomials (e.g., linear or higher-order functions) that span the space of poss`ible solutions. (는 미리 정해진 함수들이다.) Variational FormulationMultiply the PDE by a test function and integrate over an element The test function v is chosen from the same function space as the trial functions . Why use the test function ( v )?(시험함수 v는 기저 함수들 중 하나로 선택될 수 있음.)The equation:[\int*{\Omega_e} -
abla^2 u v , dx = \int*{\Omega_e} f v , dx]involves multiplying by a test function ( v ). The reason for this is to construct ( N+1 ) equations to solve ( N+1 ) unknowns in the problem (as seen in Equation 2.3). By using ( N+1 ) different test functions, you obtain ( N+1 ) linearly independent equations. Transforming to weak formThe requirement that ( u ) must be twice differentiable in the strong form is restrictive. To relax this condition, the equation is rewritten in its weak form, where the test and trial functions only need to be once differentiable. Using Gauss-Green’s lemmaApplying Gauss-Green’s theorem, the equation can be reformulated as:[\int*{\Omega_e} -
abla^2 u v , dx = \int*{\Omegae} 
abla u \cdot 
abla v , dx - \int{\partial \Omegae}  rac{\partial u}{\partial n} v , dS]The boundary term ( \int{\partial \Omegae}  rac{\partial u}{\partial n} v , dS ) often vanishes due to boundary conditions, simplifying the equation to:[\int{\Omega_e} 
abla u \cdot 
abla v , dx] ConclusionThis process is fundamental to the finite element method. Transforming to the weak form makes the problem easier to solve numerically by reducing the differentiability requirements on the solution and test functions.

posts.title: 3. Function approximation by global functions
posts.text: 1. Finite Element Method, FEM Form of ApproximationThe general strategy for approximating the solution u(x) to a differential equation is to express it as a sum of basis functions Principle for Constructing EquationsThe next step in solving such an equation is constructing a system of N+1 equations to solve for the coefficients C0, C1,… CN The Solve PhaseOnce the equations are constructed, the final step is solving the system for the unknown coefficients. Introduction to the Finite Element Method (FEM)In the context of FEM, a lot of mathematical machinery comes into play when constructing these equations. (ex. integration by parts, boundary conditions) A Simplified Approach Where is a given function. 3.1 Aproximation of vectors The least squares methodFinding Ci such that the difference , measured in a certain norm, is minimized. Projection = Galerkin’s methodFinding u such that the error is orthogonal to the space where u lies. Interpolation or collocation The regression method 3.1.1 Approximation of planar vectors The least squares methodProblem DescriptionWe aim to approximate a given vector in the direction of . Vector Space DefinitionV is one-dimensional vector space consisting of scalar multiples of We want to find the best approximation of in V, represented as , where the “best approximation” minimizes the length of the error vector . Norm of a VectorThe length of the error vector is defined as: Least Squares MethodThe energy funtion as: Here’s what each term represents: ( (f, f) ): This is the inner product (or dot product) of the vector ( f ) with itself. The inner product represents the magnitude (or energy) of the vector ( f ), and ( (f, f) ) is the squared norm of ( f ), i.e., ( |f|^2 ). It measures how large ( f ) is. ( -2c_0 (f, \psi_0) ): This term involves the inner product of ( f ) and ( \psi_0 ), scaled by the factor ( -2c_0 ). The inner product ( (f, \psi_0) ) measures how much ( f ) and ( \psi_0 ) are aligned, or how similar they are. The factor ( c_0 ) is usually the optimization parameter, and it adjusts this alignment. ( c_0^2 (\psi_0, \psi_0) ): This term involves ( c_0^2 ) multiplied by the inner product of ( \psi_0 ) with itself. The value ( (\psi_0, \psi_0) ) is the squared norm (or energy) of ( \psi_0 ), and the factor ( c_0^2 ) scales this term. As ( c_0 ) increases, this term increases as well. To minimize a function ( E(c_0) ) with respect to the parameter ( c_0 ). The reason it appears in a quadratic form is because of its similarity to problems in least squares or linear regression, where you often want to minimize the square of differences between two terms. Optimization:Since ( E(c_0) ) is a quadratic function in terms of ( c_0 ), we can find its minimum by differentiating it with respect to ( c_0 ) and setting the derivative equal to zero. The steps would look like this: Differentiate ( E(c_0) ): Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical point: Solve for ( c_0 ): Which in the present case, with , results in The projection methodWe shall now show that minimizing implies that e is orthogonal to any vector v in the space V.Then we see mathmatically that e is orthogonal to any vector v in the space V.Let be any vector, expressible as for any scalar parameter s. Then we calculate the inner product Therefore, instead of minimizing the square of the norm, we could demand that e is orthogonal to any vector in V. or REVIEWFor inner product spaces:(, ) = , Minimizing implies finding such that 3.1.2 Approximation of general vectorsGiven some vector f, we want to find the best approximation to this vector in the space V. are scalar coefficients to be determined. Least Squares Method $$E(c0, \dots, c_N) = (f, f) - 2 \sum{j=0}^N cj (f, \psi_j) + \sum{p=0}^N \sum_{q=0}^N c_p c_q (\psi_p, \psi_q)$$ Rearranging the first term to the right-hand side shows that the equation is actually a linear system for the unknown parameters , , : where:

Optimization:Since ( E(c_0) ) is a quadratic function in terms of ( c_0 ), we can find its minimum by differentiating it with respect to ( c_0 ) and setting the derivative equal to zero. The steps would look like this: Differentiate ( E(c_0) ): Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical point: Solve for ( c_0 ): Which in the present case, with , results in The projection methodWe shall now show that minimizing implies that e is orthogonal to any vector v in the space V.Then we see mathmatically that e is orthogonal to any vector v in the space V.Let be any vector, expressible as for any scalar parameter s. Then we calculate the inner product Therefore, instead of minimizing the square of the norm, we could demand that e is orthogonal to any vector in V. or 3.1.2 Approximation of general vectors

posts.title: 4. Function approximation by finite elements
posts.text: 4.1. Finite element basis functionsWe shall now turn our attention to basis functions that have compact support, meaning that they are nonzero on a small portion of only. Moreover, we shall restrict the functions to be piecewise polynominals. 4.1.1 Elements and nodesLet u and f be defined on an interval . We devide into is an element, and set of points are called nodes. Local node are numbered as i = 0, … . Global node numbers appear under the nodes, but local node numbers are not shown. 4.1.5 Example on cubic finite element functionsPiecewise cubic basis functions acn be defined by introducing four nodes per element. We see that all the piecewise linear basis functions have the same hat shape.

posts.title: 2. Mathematical preliminaries
posts.text: 2.1 ConventionsT : arbitrary-order tensor. 2.2 Definition of a tensorWe can construct tensors of arbitary order n by concatenating n bases using the tensor product . For instance, a second-order tensor results from: 2.3. Tensor calculusLet’s go over the cross product definition, the role of the Levi-Civita symbol, and how they are used together to express the cross product of two vectors. 2.3.1. Cross Product DefinitionThe cross product Is perpendicular (two lines, planes, or vectors that meet at a right angle (90 degrees)) to both and The cross product in terms of vector components is given by: Levi-Civita Symbol The Levi-Civita symbol is used to simplify expressions involving cross products in three dimensions. $$\mathbf{u} \times \mathbf{v}i = \epsilon{ijk} u_j v_k$$ 2.3.5 Tensor analysis1. Gradient of a Tensor FieldThe gradient of a function is the collection of its partial derivatives. When we apply the gradient operator to a tensor field, the order of the resulting tensor increases by one. 1.1 Partial Derivative $$\frac{\partial (\cdot)}{\partial xi} = (\cdot){,i}$$ 2. Divergence Operator The divergence of a vector or tensor field is defined. The divergence measures the “spread” or “flux” of a vector field. Divergence of a Vector Field $$\text{div}u = \nabla \cdot u = \frac{\partial ui}{\partial x_i} = u{i,i}$$

posts.title: 4. Stress
posts.text: Before this, we only talked about deformation.This chapter focuses on the notion of force!We introduce the quantitiy of stress briefly in a phenomenological way. The goal of this section rather is to define stress quantities frequently used in nonlinear continuum mechanics besides the well known Cuachy stress. *(Cauchy stress tensor completely defines the state of stress at a point inside a material in the deformed state, placement, or configuration.) 4.1 Phenomenological definition of stressThe continuum body is cur in two pieces and an infinitesimal surface element defined by its magnitude and its normal unit vector is depicted in the reference configuration together with the corresponding surface element described by and in the current deformed configuration. We can assume an infinitesimal force acting on the surface element . With respect to this force we define the so called Cauchy traction (코우시 당김) : At the same time, knowing the surface element corresponding to in its undeformend state , we define the so called first Piola-Kirchhoff traction vector depicted in Summed up: 4.2 Reference configuration stress tensor

posts.title: 3. Kinematics
posts.text: 3.1 Configuration and motion spatialA spatial domain (오메가는 공간영역) and is supposed to be stress free. The reference configuration (기준 구성) is . The current configuration (현재 구성) at time . In nonlinear continuum mechanics denotes the time interval of interest. Note that a fixed for all times points to a specific material element. Therefore can directly be identified with material elements. Furthermore, assuming that the deformation map is invertible. Lagrangian description: mechnaical physics -> reference configuration, material coordinates.Following whole path of the displacement. Eulerian description: fluid mechanics -> current configuration, spatial coordinates.We observing a space, see what configuration and form the materials that pass the space. 3.2 Displacement, velocity and accelerationA mathmatical description for the motion of a continuum (Lagangian): A spatial coodinates for the motion of a continuum (Eulerian): The Material VelocityThe material velocity field can be computed by the first partial derivative of the deformation map with respect to time: The Material Aceleration 3.3 Material derivativeMaterial Derivative:The material derivative represents the rate of change of a physical quantity observed from the perspective of a moving material element. It’s especially useful when the deformation map is not known. The current configuration acceleration field of material elements was deduced by a transformation of the reference configuration acceleration field. Computing the material derivative The material derivative is a general operator used to describe the rate of change of physical quantity as experienced by a material element moving with the flow. : The local rate of change of the quantity in time, observed at a fixed point in space. : The convective rate of change, which accounts for how the movement of the material element through space alters the quantity due to spatial variations. Example Local rate of change (국부적 변화율): 공간의 한 점에서 시간에 따라 온도가 변하는 속도(즉, ). 예: 태양열에 의해 특정 지점에서의 온도가 선형적으로 증가하는 상황. 그래프의 기울기 는 온도가 시간에 따라 증가하는 정도를 보여줍니다. Convective rate of change (대류적 변화율): 강물의 흐름에 따라 온도가 이동하며 변하는 속도(즉,). 강물이 이동하면서 새 물질 입자(예: 물 분자)가 관찰 지점으로 들어오고, 이 입자는 원래 위치에서의 온도를 가져옵니다. 이로 인해 온도의 변화는 강물의 흐름 속도 와 온도의 공간 변화율 에 의존합니다. 3.4 Deformation gradient

posts.title: 1. Classical Mechanics
posts.text: 1.1 Microscopic Laws of MotionPhase space and Trajectory $$F_i(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_N) = m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_i \quad \forall i = 1, \ldots, N$$$F_i$ is the force acting on particle i due to all other particles in the system.It’s a force-centric description. 1.2 Lagrangian formulationThe lagrangian L is defined in terms of kinetic K and potential energy U as$$L(\mathbf{r}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_N, \dot{\mathbf{r}}_1, \ldots, \dot{\mathbf{r}}_N) = K(\dot{\mathbf{r}}_1, \ldots, \dot{\mathbf{r}}_N) - U(\mathbf{r}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_N)$$It’s a energy centric description Kinetic EnergyThe kinetic energy of a single particle is defined as a work needed to accelerate the particle from rest velocity.For the N-particle system, the kinetic energy is thereore given by$$K = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{1}{2} m_i \dot{\mathbf{r}}_i^2$$ Potential Energy and Conservative ForcesA conservative force is a force that can be derived from a scalar function called potential energy$$\mathbf{F}_i(\mathbf{r}_1, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N) = - \frac{\partial U(\mathbf{r}_1, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N)}{\partial \mathbf{r}_i}$$ The negative sign indicates that the force acts in the direction that decreases the potential energy. This is a reflection of how systems tend to move towards configurations that minimize their potential energy. Generalized CoordinatesA cartesian coordinates ${r_1, \dots, r_N}$ to generalized coordinates ${q_1, \dots, q_3N}$, where we assume that there is a unique coordinate transformation from one the other.Also for a system with N unconstrained particles, we have 3N configurational degrees of freedom. However for $N_c$ holonomic constraints,$$f_j(q_1, \dots, q_{3N}, t) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad j = 1, \dots, N_c$$Then the system has $3N - N_c$ configurational degrees of freedom. In generalized coordinates, the Lagrangian reads$$L(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - U(q)$$ex) We can perform a coordinate transformation form r = (x, y) to the generalized coordinates $q = {r, \theta}$ thus, $y = - r \cos \theta$

posts.title: 3. From Statistical Mechanics to Thermodynamics
posts.text: 3.1 Liouville’s the theorem and equationThe total number of ensemble members is constant. So phase space volume must be equal to the rate at which the ensemble members leave through the surface S. This is exactly same conditon that we impose to derive the mass continuity equation. ex) Phase Space Equation ex) Fluid Dynamics Equation In statistical mechanics, we consider the phase space, which is a 6𝑁 dimensional space for a system with 𝑁 particles. Each point in this space is represented by: where: are the position coordinates. are the momentum coordinates. We can imagine as “velocity” in the phase space. be the ensemble density in phase spaceSo the continuity equation in phase space is .. where: is the divergence operator in the -dimensional phase space. represents the probability current in phase space. 1. Liouville equationHamiltonian dynamics can be described as an incompressible flow in phase space. 2. Liouville Operator and Poisson Brackets Liouville equation II is the Liouville operator, which can be defined using Possion brackets. Poisson bracket Thus, Liouville Equation is If the phase space distribution function (\rho(Z, t)) is conserved, it implies: Phase Space Volume Preservation 3.2 Statistical Ensembles in Equilibrium Equilibrium ConditionFor a system to be in equilibrium, the phase space distribution function should not explicitly depend on time: $\ Ergodic HypothesisThe ergodic hypothesis states that, given an infinite amount of time, an ergodic system will eventually visit all possible states that are accessible to it. For Hamiltonian systems, this implies that the system will cover all points on a hypersurface where the Hamiltonian ( H ) is constant. Ensemble and Temporal AverageIn an ergodic system, the ensemble average of a quantity is equivalent to its temporal average types of statistical ensembles The types of statistical ensembles are classified based on the quantities held constant:ex) Microcanonical Ensemble (): Represents an isolated system with fixed particle number (N), volume (V), and energy (E). The system follows Hamiltonian dynamics, and the total energy is conserved. Canonical Ensemble (): Represents a system in contact with a heat bath at a fixed temperature . The total energy of the system is not conserved because it can exchange energy with the heat bath. 3.3 Basic ThermodynamicsDefinitionsA thermodynamics system is a macroscopic system. The thermodynamic state of the system is uniquely defined by specification of a set of control variables, e.g, temperature, pressure, volume etc. Two types of thermodynamic variables: extensive variables are proportional to the system size (ex. internal energy, mass, entropy) intensive variables are independent of the system size (ex. pressure, temperature, density, chemial potential) 3.4 Laws of Thermodynamics Zeroth Law (0법칙)Establishes the concept of temperature and thermal equilibrium. If two systems are in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are also in equilibrium with each other. First Law (1법칙)The conservation energy. The energy E of the system can be changed by performing work W on it and/or by adding heat Q to it Second Law (2법칙) Third LawThe entropy of a system at the absolute zero temperature is a universal constant, which we can set to zero. The T = 0K temperature can never be physically reached. 3.5 Comparing 4 methods 1st order Euler 1st order Cromer 2nd order Runge-Kutta 2nd order Velocity Verlet Hamilton’s Eqations: Symplectic Structure:Hamilton’s equations preserve the structure of the phase space In matrix terms, this can be represented by the structure of the evolution equations, where M is a matrix expressible in block form as is the identity matrix. is the zero matrix. Jacobian Matrix J: . Dynamical systems for which${M = J_{T}MJ} have sympletic property. Since det M = 1, it follows that det J = 1.The Hamiltonian is H = \dot{\mathbf{r}} = \frac{\partial H}{\partial \mathbf{p}} = \frac{\mathbf{p}}{m} \dot{\mathbf{p}} = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial \mathbf{r}} = -\frac{\partial U(\mathbf{r})}{\partial \mathbf{r}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r}) (\mathbf{r}) is the position vector. (\mathbf{p}) is the momentum vector. (H(\mathbf{r}, \mathbf{p})) is the Hamiltonian (total energy). (\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{r})) is the force, typically given by (\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U(\mathbf{r})). Question 1: 1D harmonic oscillatorm: massk: a linear spring with force constantx: oscillating direction a) Write down the Lagrangian, Hamiltonian and the Hamilton’s equations LagrangianL = T - VT: the kinetic energyV: the potential energy The kinetic energy: The potential energy: The Lagrangian is: Hamiltonian where p is the generalized momentum: Hamilton’s Equations of motion The deformation map is given by: represents the material coodinates, and represents the spatial coodinates. b) Write down the update rules for this problem for all 4 integrators.

posts.title: 2. From Classical to Statistical Mechanics
posts.text: 2.1 Lagrangian formulationPrinciple of Stationary Action To derive the equation of motion in Lagrange formulation we will use the principle of stationary that states: the physical path that a given system will take between two points is the one for which the action S is an extremum (극점). The action integral is $$S = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} L\left[\mathbf{q}(t), \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t)\right] , dt$$ Mathematically condition for the extremum of S is $\delta S = 0$. $$\delta S = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} \sum_{\alpha=1}^{3N} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_\alpha} - \frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_\alpha} \right) \right) \delta q_\alpha(t) , dt$$ The first term in third line cancels due to the constraint $\delta q(t_0) = \delta q(t_1) = 0$.Since variations $\delta q_\alpha(t)$ are independent and arbitrary the requirement $\delta S = 0$ can only be satisfied when the term in brackets vanishes independently for all $\alpha$. This leads to the Euler-Lagrange equation. $$\frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_\alpha} \right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_\alpha} = 0 \quad (\alpha = 1, \dots, 3N)$$ Equivalance with Newton’s second law Conserved QuantitiesThe equations of motion in the Lagrangian formulation are given by the Euler-Lagrance equation:$$\frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_\alpha} \right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_\alpha} = 0 \quad (\alpha = 1, \dots, 3N)$$ 2.2 Hamiltonian formulationThe Hamiltonian 𝐻 provides a more accurate description of a system’s energy changes when external forces are acting on it because it explicitly incorporates both momentum and position (which are affected by external forces) in its formulation. Unlike the total energy 𝐸, which can only describe the energy in terms of position and velocity, the Hamiltonian also captures the impact of external influences, making it a more complete and dynamic tool for understanding energy changes in systems with external forces. The Hamiltonian fuction is.. $$H(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{p}) = \sum_{\alpha=1}^{3N} p_\alpha \dot{q}_\alpha - L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$$ Here is the equation written out: $p_i$ is the generalized momentum $q$ is the generalized coordinate U(q) is the potential energy $$H({\mathbf{r}i}, {\mathbf{p}_i}) = \sum{i=1}^N \mathbf{p}i \cdot \mathbf{r}_i - L({\mathbf{r}_i}, {\dot{\mathbf{r}}_i})= \sum{i=1}^N mi \dot{\mathbf{r}}_i^2 - \frac{1}{2} \sum{i=1}^N mi \dot{\mathbf{r}}_i^2 + U({\mathbf{r}_i})= \frac{1}{2} \sum{i=1}^N mi \dot{\mathbf{r}}_i^2 + U({\mathbf{r}_i})= \sum{i=1}^N \frac{\mathbf{p}_i^2}{2 m_i} + U({\mathbf{r}_i})$$ Hamiltonian, therefore, corresponds to the total energy E of the system.H = K + U = E This property constrains are phase space vector to a (6N - 1)-dimensional constrant-energy hypersurface. Legrendre TransformationThe Hamiltonian can be obtained from the Lagrangian via the Legrendre Transform. The Legendre transform is defined as$$F(y) = yx(y) - f[x(y)]$$. In order to verify that F(y) is a function of y not x, we differentiate$$dF = d(xy) - df$$= xdy + yds - ydx= xdy $$x = - dF/ dy$$ The Legendre transform can be alternatively written as F(y) = -[yx - f(x)].In this case, we have $x = - \delta F / \delta y$. More generally for a function of several variables, $$F(y_1, \dots, y_K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k x_k(y_1, \dots, y_K) - f[x_1(y_1, \dots, y_K), \dots, x_K(y_1, \dots, y_K)]$$ To obtain the Hamiltonian from the Lagrangian we do a change of variables so that the Hamiltonian is a function of generalized coordinates and conjugate(활용) momentum.$$L(q, \dot{q}) \to H(q, p)$$ In Hamiltonian formulation, we treate $q_a, p_a$ as independent variables.$$dH = \sum_{a=1}^{3N} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial q_a} dq_a + \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_a} dp_a \right)$$ On the other hand, by differentiating we obtain,$$dH = \sum_{a=1}^{3N} \left( d(p_a \dot{q}_a) - dL \right)$$ $$= \sum_{a=1}^{3N} \left( \dot{q}_a dp_a + p_a d\dot{q}_a - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_a} dq_a - \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_a} d\dot{q}_a \right)$$ $$= \sum_{a=1}^{3N} \left( -\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_a} dq_a + \dot{q}_a dp_a \right)$$ $$= \sum_{a=1}^{3N} \left( -\frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_a} \right) dq_a + \dot{q}_a dp_a \right)$$ $$= \sum_{a=1}^{3N} \left( -p_a dq_a + \dot{q}_a dp_a \right)$$ $$\dot{q}_a = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_a}, \quad \dot{p}_a = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_a}$$ In Cartiesian coordinates, $$\dot{r}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i} = \frac{p_i}{m_i} = v_i,p$$ $$\dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial r_i} = -\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r_i} = F_i \implies m_i \ddot{r}_i = F_i$$ 2.3 Ensemble ConceptTo reduce the high dimensionalty of the problem, we introduce a statistical description of the problem via ensemble concept. An ensemble could be a collection of M systems each containing N particles and evolving under the same microscopic interactions but starting from different initial conditions. Ensemble Distribution Fuction The microstates of an ensemble do not occupy the total phase space because they correspond to a particular macroscopic set of observables (관측 값 집합에 해당하는 부분만 차지).$\rho(Z, t)$ describes the likelihood of finding a system at time t in state Z. It is a probability distribution fuction. (시간 t에서 상태 Z에 있을 확률 밀도) $$\rho(Z, t) \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{\Gamma} \rho(Z, t)dZ = 1$$ Hypervolume $$dZ = \prod_{a=1}^{3N} dq_a dp_a$$ Ensemble AverageA macroscopic observable of the ensemble A is calculated by performing an average over the M systems in the ensemble: $$A = \frac{1}{M} \sum*{\lambda=1}^M a(Z*\lambda) \equiv \langle a \rangle$$ $$A = \langle a \rangle = \int_{\Gamma} a(Z) \rho(Z, t)dZ. \tag$$ A is an expectation value with respect to the phase space probability distribution function $\rho(Z, t)$.

posts.title: 4. Microcanonical Ensemble
posts.text: 4.1 Postulate of Equal A priori ProbabilityThe microcanonical ensemble is build upon the postulate of equal a priori probability which states that all microscopic states in an ensemble are equally probable. It describes a closed isolated system in equilibrium following Hamiltonian dynamics, these microscopic states lie on a constant energy hypersurface A phase space distribution function that reflacts this considerations(공간상 분포 함수: 주어진 조건 하에서 특정 상태가 위상 공간에서 얼마나 가능성 있는지를 나타낸다.) is *Partition function is The partition function serves as a mathematical tool that bridges microscopic properties with macroscopic thermodynamic quantities. Also we have to consider the renormalization factors that are quantum mechanical in nature. The factor N! accounts for the permutations of particles, while the factor is due to the quantum uncertainty in position and momenta: So we rewrite the partition function as Boltzmann’s relationLudwig Boltzmann identified the connection between the number of accessible microstates and the entropy as EX) Ideal Gas 1. Hamiltonian of the ideal Gas system (p_i): The momentum of the (i)-th particle. (m): The mass of each particle. We notice that the constant energy hypersurface is a 3N-dimension sphere with radius Phase Volume Volume is Thus, The microcanonical partition function is Thus, Sackur-Tetrode equation is Applied Stirling’s Approximation!n! \approx n \ln n - n + 1/2 \ln (2\pi n) S: Entropy of the system. N: Number of particles. : Boltzmann constant. V: Volume of the gas. m: Mass of a single gas particle. E: Total energy of the gas. h: Planck’s constant. 4.2 Thermodynamics of Microcanonical EnsembleA microcanonical ensemble is closed system. The variables that uniquely describe the state of the system are number of particles N, volume V, energy E. (열과 일을 환경과 교환 X) The state function is entropy, S = S(N, V, E) First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics CombinedT: Temperaturep: Pressure: Chemical potential We have used the relation for quasi-static mechanical work dW - pdV and also considered a chemical work of particle insertion . Total Differential of Entropy (S): $$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial E}\right){N, V} dE + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right){E, N} dV + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial N}\right)_{E, V} dN$$ By comparing the dS, dE we identify the relations$$\frac{1}{T} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial E}\right){N, V}, \quad \frac{p}{T} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right){E, N}, \quad -\frac{\mu}{T} = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial N}\right)_{E, V}$$ By comparing dE, dS (E = E(S, V, N)), we obtain$$T = \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial S}\right){N, V}, \quad -p = \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial V}\right){S, N}, \quad \mu = \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial N}\right)_{S, V}$$ EX) Ideal Gas LawUsing statistical mechanics and thermodynamics we induce From the Sackur-Tetrode equation From the theomodynamic relation While from the relation for p, Maxwell’s RelationsThermodynamics relations can also be obtained from Maxwell’s relations. Gibbs-Duhem Relation A System that is composed of subsystems, each with variables E, S, V, N.For the total system,
